notable.notable_blurb: Harvard sociologist; father of structural functionalism

narrative_blocks.detail: Talcott Parsons's mother Mary Augusta Ingersoll (HD1854) was the daughter of George Lyman Ingersoll and Catharine L. Talcott — a descendent of T01001 Samuel Talcott and through him of the Hartford founder John Talcott. The name "Talcott" was chosen in honor of that line, making Talcott Parsons simultaneously an 9th-generation Hooker descendant and a direct link between the two founding Hartford families that this database tracks. His father Edward Smith Parsons was a Yale Divinity graduate and Congregational minister — the Puritan tradition running directly to Harvard's leading sociologist.
narrative_blocks.body: His name "Talcott" honored his maternal great-grandmother Catharine L. Talcott, great-great-great-great-granddaughter of John Talcott, Hartford founder. He was himself a 9th-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Theorized that Hooker's Puritanism created American democracy
narrative_blocks.detail: Parsons maintained that Calvinist radicalism, as expressed in New England Puritanism, established something unique in the world: "radical views on individuality, egalitarianism, skepticism toward state power, and the zeal of the religious calling." He traced American democracy and exceptionalism directly to the Puritan Revolution. The extraordinary irony: Rev. Thomas Hooker founded Hartford CT in 1636 by leading his congregation out of Massachusetts to establish a civil covenant. His 9th-generation descendant — carrying both the Hooker and Talcott blood of Hartford's founding generation — became the 20th century's foremost theorist of how that very Puritan act shaped all of American civilization.
narrative_blocks.body: Parsons argued that Calvinist-Puritan value patterns — the tradition of Thomas Hooker himself — became institutionalized as the foundational "American value system."

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard's most influential — and most criticized — sociologist
narrative_blocks.detail: Parsons joined Harvard's newly created sociology department in 1930 and helped found the Department of Social Relations. His translations of Max Weber introduced Weber's thought to English-speaking scholarship. He was president of the American Sociological Association in 1949. His structural functionalism dominated American sociology for three decades before being widely criticized in the 1970s as too abstract and socially conservative. FBI director J. Edgar Hoover authorized a security investigation of Parsons in 1952 on accusations of communist sympathies. Parsons responded: "This allegation is so preposterous that I cannot understand how any reasonable person could come to the conclusion that I was a member of the Communist Party or ever had been."
narrative_blocks.body: One of the most famous sociologists in the world from the 1940s to the 1970s; translated Max Weber into English; founding member of Harvard's sociology department; investigated by FBI during McCarthy era.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage united Hooker and Talcott family lines
narrative_blocks.body: George Lyman Ingersoll married Catharine 'Kate' L. Talcott on January 12, 1855 in Lanesboro Massachusetts. Kate was the great-great-granddaughter of Samuel Talcott — making their marriage a documented Hooker-Talcott family-line convergence within the tree. Their three children Mary Augusta, George Talcott, and Edwards Platt inherited both lines; Mary Augusta's grandson Talcott Parsons (1902-1979) — the major American sociologist — was named 'Talcott' to honor this maternal Talcott family.

George Lyman Ingersoll began his career as a printer/publisher at Hudson Ohio — the firm Sauver & Ingersoll published 'some of the earliest books in Ohio' and George edited the Ohio Observer for Western Reserve College Professor Henry W. Day. He moved to Cleveland in 1857, studied law, and joined the firm Ingersoll Brothers with his brother Jonathan Edwards Ingersoll. The press-to-bar career arc was characteristic of mid-nineteenth-century midwestern professional formation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Cleveland Presbyterian churches over career
narrative_blocks.body: George Ingersoll was connected successively to three Cleveland Presbyterian congregations across his career — Euclid Avenue Presbyterian, then Cass Avenue Presbyterian, then Beckwith Memorial Church — serving as deacon, treasurer, and session member. The serial congregational moves reflected the typical residential and pastoral shifts of growing Gilded Age Cleveland, where the city's Protestant denominational architecture was being established and rebuilt across decades.

George Lyman Ingersoll was born February 12 or 13, 1830 in Rochester New York (some records say Buffalo) and attended school in Rochester and at Oberlin Ohio. After Kate's death in 1870 he remarried Cornelia Howard Saunders on July 11, 1871 in Cleveland. He died July 28, 1910 in Cleveland Ohio at about age eighty.

INST018.name: Amherst College
INST018.notes: Founded 1821. One of the oldest liberal arts colleges in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 6th President 1890-1899. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amherst_College David Peck Todd (HD1758) was astronomy professor and observatory director 1881-1917. Millicent Todd Bingham (HD1759) received honorary LL.D. 1957.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
